explanation: The Sigma rule looks for the literal string 'DCLCWPDTSD' within the command line. By using PowerShell string concatenation, the malicious SDDL string is constructed at runtime, preventing a static match against the CommandLine field while still achieving the same functional result.

Assigning the sensitive SDDL component to a variable before passing it to the cmdlet breaks the 'contains|all' logic of the Sigma rule, as the command line will contain the variable assignment rather than the direct application of the flagged string to the parameter.
This uses character array manipulation and a replacement function to hide the signature 'DCLCWPDTSD' from simple command-line logging and pattern matching.

The Sigma rule is specifically scoped to detect 'Set-Service' via 'pwsh.exe' (selection_img). Using the native Windows binary 'sc.exe' to set the Security Descriptor (sdset) bypasses the image name requirement and the PowerShell-specific cmdlet detection logic entirely.
